note_data.title: Water
keyPoints: **Water supply** is the amount of water available from sources such as rivers, lakes, groundwater and precipitation
**Water demand** increases due to population growth, urbanisation, industrialisation and improved living standards
**Water stress** occurs when demand exceeds supply, leading to shortages and competition between users
**Dams** are built across rivers to store water for irrigation, hydroelectric power generation and domestic supply
**Multipurpose schemes** like the Indus Basin Project provide irrigation, flood control, hydroelectric power and water storage
**Groundwater depletion** occurs when water is extracted faster than natural recharge, causing water tables to fall
**Water pollution** reduces available clean water supply through contamination from sewage, industrial waste and agricultural chemicals
**Water conservation** methods include rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation, recycling and reducing wastage
**Conflicts over water** arise between countries sharing river basins and between different user groups

definitions.definition: The amount of water available from natural sources such as rivers, lakes, groundwater and precipitation
The amount of water required by people for domestic, agricultural and industrial purposes
A barrier built across a river valley to control water flow and create a reservoir for water storage
A large-scale water management project that serves several purposes such as irrigation, flood control, hydroelectric power and water supply
A situation where water demand exceeds the available water supply in a region

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed facts about what something is like or how it works
Give reasonable ideas with brief explanations

modelAnswer.question: Explain why water demand is increasing in many developing countries. [6 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Population growth increases water demand because more people need water for drinking, washing and cooking. Urbanisation increases demand because cities concentrate large populations in small areas requiring piped water supply. Industrialisation increases demand because factories need water for manufacturing processes and cooling. Improved living standards increase demand because people use more water for washing machines, showers and gardens. Agricultural development increases demand because irrigation is needed to grow more crops to feed the growing population. Economic development increases demand because as countries become wealthier, people consume more water-intensive products and services.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Industrialisation requires water for processes

commonMistakes: Never say 'water scarcity' when the question asks about supply - always distinguish between supply (availability) and demand (requirements)
Students often confuse dams with barrages - dams store water in reservoirs, barrages divert water flow
Don't just list benefits of dams - always explain the problems such as displacement of people and environmental impacts
Never say water is 'dirty' - use specific terms like 'contaminated', 'polluted' or 'unsafe'
Students often forget to mention both physical and human factors affecting water supply
examinerTips: This topic appears frequently in Paper 2 questions worth 6-8 marks
Always use specific examples like Tarbela Dam in Pakistan or Aswan High Dam in Egypt
Draw and label diagrams of dams showing reservoir, spillway and power station for full marks
Case studies must include both benefits and problems of water management schemes
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Cross-section diagram of a multipurpose dam showing: dam wall, reservoir, spillway, hydroelectric power station, irrigation canals, and flood control mechanisms. Label all components and show water flow directions.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2020 P2 Q2a (2 marks) — they asked students to describe the state of water on different surfaces as shown in a diagram
This topic was tested in O/N 2019 P1 Q4c (4 marks) — they asked students to state the depth of groundwater at a specific point using a diagram with measurements
This topic was tested in M/J 2021 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — they asked students to compare water content and rate of movement for different mass movement processes
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P2 Q6 (15 marks) — they asked students to evaluate the importance of water processes in developing landforms in hot arid environments
This topic was tested in M/J 2018 P1 Q3b (3 marks) — they asked students to identify which regions would need most irrigation water using a climate map

workedExample.question: State the depth of groundwater at X in Fig. 1.1. (2 marks)
steps: Step 1: Locate point X on the diagram and identify the water table line
Step 2: Read the scale on the y-axis to measure distance from ground surface to water table at point X
Step 3: Record the measurement range accounting for reading accuracy
Step 4: Include appropriate units (metres) in your answer
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct numerical range (5.0-9.9), 1 mark for including units (metres) and reference to below ground level

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to include units when stating groundwater depths' — always include metres and specify 'below ground level' for depth measurements
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often confused the three states of water' — always distinguish clearly between liquid, solid/frozen, and gas/vapour when describing water states

Examiner Report 2021: 'Students gave vague answers about water processes without specific examples' — always name specific processes like hydraulic action, abrasion, or solution when discussing water's role in landform development
note_data.keyDiagram: Water cycle diagram showing: evaporation from oceans/lakes (with arrows), condensation in clouds, precipitation as rain/snow, surface runoff into rivers, infiltration into soil, percolation to groundwater, water table line, and transpiration from vegetation. Must label all processes and show directional arrows clearly. Include sun as energy source and distinguish between different water stores (atmospheric, surface, groundwater).
note_data.quickRecap: Water exists in three states: liquid, solid (ice/frozen), and gas (water vapour). It moves through the water cycle via evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, and infiltration. Groundwater depth is measured from surface to water table in metres below ground level. Water processes like hydraulic action and solution are crucial in shaping landforms, especially in arid environments where occasional flash floods cause rapid erosion.
